context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on Problem Set 4. Could you just explain the whole beaded-string / normal-modes idea from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I'm here to help you work through the problems, not to re-teach the whole topic from scratch — that's what your lectures and textbook are for!

Let's start with Problem 4.1. Before we dive in: what does it mean physically for a bead to be at the wall? What constraint does the wall impose on the bead's motion?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the whole topic rather than engaging with a specific part of the problem. My role is to guide them through the problems, not to lecture. I should redirect them to the actual assignment and use the Socratic method to get them started on Problem 4.1 specifically. I'll ask a targeted opening question to diagnose what they already know and get them moving.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure — I just know the wall is fixed, but then the bead isn’t actually on the wall and that’s where I get confused. The a/2 thing and the fake extra bead idea are kind of mixing together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you're right that the wall is fixed. So let's focus on that first.

The wall sits at distance a/2 from bead 1. If you imagine the string continuing past the wall, what would the displacement of the wall itself have to be at all times?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating two things: the physical boundary condition (wall is fixed) and the mathematical trick (imaginary bead). I should separate these two ideas by first pinning down what the wall actually does physically, before introducing the mirror/image bead concept. One focused Socratic question is enough.

exchanges.student: I guess the wall displacement is always 0, but then I don’t see how that turns into \(A_1=-A_0\). Can you just walk me through that whole mirror-bead thing more directly?
exchanges.tutor: Good, the wall displacement is always zero. Now think about this: the wall sits exactly halfway between bead 0 (imaginary) and bead 1 (real). If you need the midpoint between two values to always be zero, what relationship must those two values have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student knows the wall displacement is zero but wants me to explain the mirror-bead trick directly. I should not give it away. Instead, I'll use a Socratic question to bridge the gap: if the wall displacement is zero, and the wall sits exactly halfway between where bead 0 (imaginary) and bead 1 (real) would be, what mathematical condition on their amplitudes would make the midpoint always zero? This nudges them toward the answer without giving it.

exchanges.student: I think they have to be opposites, but I’m still not seeing the bigger picture at all. I’m probably confused about all of 4.1, especially how that boundary condition turns into the actual normal modes.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — opposites. So if the imaginary bead 0 has amplitude A0, what does that tell you about A1? Can you write that as an equation?

Once you've got that, part (b) asks the same question for the right-hand wall — try applying the same logic there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that the two values must be opposites, which is the key insight for part (a). I should affirm this and close that step, then redirect them to write it as A1 = -A0 themselves. I should not jump ahead to normal modes yet — that's part (c). Let me get them to consolidate part (a) first, then naturally move to part (b) before tackling (c).

exchanges.student: So I’d write \(A_1=-A_0\), and I’m guessing on the right side it’s something like \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\). But after that I’m stuck again — I don’t know how to go from those endpoint conditions to the allowed modes and frequencies.
exchanges.tutor:
Both boundary conditions are correct — well done.

Now for part (c): in a beaded string, each bead's amplitude in a normal mode can be written as a function of its position. What general mathematical form would you expect the amplitude of bead n to take?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the boundary conditions for both walls. I should affirm this briefly and then move them toward part (c). The key step is connecting the boundary conditions to the sinusoidal normal mode ansatz. I should ask them what form they expect the amplitudes to take for a traveling or standing wave, nudging them toward the standard ansatz An = sin(nka) or similar, without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I don’t know if it’s sine or exponential or both. Could you maybe just set up the whole derivation once, because I’m losing track of what ansatz I’m even supposed to start with?
exchanges.tutor: I won't set it up for you, but think about this: for a normal mode, do you expect the bead displacements to grow unboundedly as you move along the string, or to oscillate? What does that tell you about sine vs. exponential?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to set up the derivation for them, which I should refuse. However, I can ask a guiding question that points them toward the right ansatz. Since they mention sine vs. exponential, I should ask them to think about what kind of solution they expect physically — oscillatory or growing/decaying — to help them choose. This is a single targeted question that moves them forward without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: Probably oscillate, so sine/cosine or \(e^{ikna}\) with oscillation. But then I’m confused again because I don’t know which version to use, and I also don’t know how the boundary conditions pick the allowed \(k\).
exchanges.tutor:
Good reasoning. Since your boundary conditions involve antisymmetry (odd function behavior), sine is the more natural choice here.

So try writing the amplitude of bead n as A_n = C·sin(nka). Now apply your two boundary conditions — what equations do you get for k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they correctly identified oscillatory solutions. They have two sub-questions: which form to use, and how boundary conditions select k. I should address the first briefly (both forms are equivalent, but sine is natural given the boundary conditions) and then ask them to apply A1 = -A0 and A_{N+1} = -A_N to their ansatz to see what constraint on k emerges. I should not do the algebra for them.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, because when I plug in \(n=1\) I get \(A_1=C\sin(ka)\), but I don’t know how to use \(A_0\) in that same form without getting 0 automatically. And then I’m worried I’m using the wrong form again.
exchanges.tutor:
Good catch! Notice that A0 = C·sin(0·ka) = 0, so the left boundary condition A1 = -A0 is automatically satisfied by the sine ansatz. 

So the real constraint on k comes from the right wall. Apply A_{N+1} = -A_N to your sine ansatz — what equation do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student noticed that A0 = C·sin(0) = 0 automatically, which actually satisfies A1 = -A0 only if A1 = 0 too, which can't be right. This is a good observation that shows they're thinking carefully. The issue is that the boundary condition A1 = -A0 is automatically satisfied by the sine ansatz (since sin(0)=0), so the real constraint comes from the RIGHT wall boundary condition. I should point them toward this insight with a question.

exchanges.student: I get \(C\sin((N+1)ka)=-C\sin(Nka)\), but I don’t know how to simplify that without messing up the trig. Can you just show that step?
exchanges.tutor: I won't show the step, but think about this: when is sin(x) = −sin(y) in general? What's the simplest relationship between x and y that makes that true?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right equation but is struggling with the trig simplification. I should not show the step for them. Instead, I can hint at using a specific trig identity — the sum-to-product identity — or ask them to think about when sin(x) = -sin(y) in general. This is a targeted nudge without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the trig identity stuff. Could you just explain this part from the start in a cleaner way, because I feel like I only know random pieces and not how they connect.
exchanges.tutor:
You have all the pieces — let's not restart. Here's the one trig fact you need: sin(x) = −sin(y) is true when x + y = mπ for integer m.

Apply that to your equation. What do you get for k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking me to re-explain from scratch. We've actually made substantial progress - they have the right ansatz, the right equation, and just need one trig fact. I should not restart. Instead, I should be direct: remind them of the simple identity sin(x) = -sin(-x), or equivalently sin(x) = -sin(π - x + π) = -sin(something). Actually the key fact is sin(θ) = -sin(-θ), so sin(x) = -sin(y) means x = -y + 2πn or x + y = 2πn or x = π + y + 2πn... I need to be careful. The key identity is sin(α) = -sin(β) implies α + β = mπ (for integer m) or α - β = (2m+1)π. Actually more precisely: sin(α) = -sin(β) = sin(-β), so either α = -β + 2mπ or α = π+β + 2mπ. Given the physical context the useful one is (N+1)ka + Nka = mπ, i.e., (2N+1)ka = mπ. I should nudge the student to recall that sin(x) = -sin(y) when x + y = mπ, without giving the full derivation.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the tutor tells the student that 'sine is the more natural choice here' and then directly provides the ansatz A_n = C·sin(nka), doing the key reasoning step of selecting and setting up the trial solution rather than letting the student derive or choose it. In Turn 8, the tutor explains that A0 = C·sin(0·ka) = 0 so the left boundary condition is automatically satisfied, again doing the interpretive reasoning step for the student instead of guiding them to see it.

The student repeatedly expresses confusion and asks to restart or be shown the derivation (Turns 6, 8, 10), and the tutor continues cycling through the same Socratic question style without consolidating progress, reframing, or switching strategy. By Turn 10, the student is still stuck on the same sub-step and the tutor has not advanced the dialogue meaningfully.

grade.overview: The tutor generally maintained a Socratic approach and avoided giving submission-ready answers. However, in Turns 7–8, the tutor crossed into doing key reasoning steps for the student by selecting the sine ansatz and explaining why the left boundary condition is automatically satisfied. Additionally, the tutor repeatedly used the same question-style approach across multiple turns without adapting strategy when the student remained stuck, constituting pattern-level repetition without synthesis.
grade.judge_reasoning: For 1.1, the tutor's provision of the sine ansatz and the explanation that A0=sin(0)=0 automatically satisfies the left boundary condition (Turns 7–8) constitutes doing the key reasoning step for the student (1.1.C.b), warranting a 6-point deduction. The full 12-point deduction for 1.1.A.a is not warranted because the tutor never produced submission-ready output. For 2.1, the student expressed confusion and requested restarts in Turns 6, 8, and 10, yet the tutor continued the same Socratic question pattern without reframing, summarizing progress, or switching strategy, triggering the 4-point deduction under 2.1.A.a. Scaffolding (1.2) was handled well—the tutor built on each correct student step. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout.
